questions.question:
Match the following:

P. Lexical analysis       1. Graph coloring
Q. Parsing                2. DFA minimization
R. Register allocation    3. Post-order traversal
S. Expression evaluation  4. Production tree

A. P-2, Q-3, R-1, S-4
B. P-2, Q-4, R-1, S-3
C. P-2, Q-4, R-3, S-1
D. P-2, Q-1, R-4, S-3
questions.to_find: Match each compiler phase with the technique/structure it primarily uses.

understand.plain: A compiler has several phases, each of which relies on a specific algorithmic technique or data structure. This question asks you to pair each phase (P, Q, R, S) with its corresponding technique (1, 2, 3, 4). You need to recall what algorithm or structure is central to each named phase.

keywords.explain: The process of reducing a Deterministic Finite Automaton to its smallest equivalent form; used in lexical analysis to build efficient scanners from regular expressions.
keywords.example: Merging equivalent states in the DFA built from a regular expression for identifiers.

keywords.explain: A tree that records how a parser applied grammar productions to derive a sentence; the result of the parsing phase.
keywords.example: Parsing 'a+b*c' produces a tree reflecting operator precedence according to the grammar rules.

keywords.explain: An algorithm used in register allocation: variables are nodes in an interference graph and colors represent registers; adjacent nodes (simultaneously live variables) must get different colors.
keywords.example: If variables x and y are live at the same time, they cannot share the same register.

keywords.explain: A tree traversal that processes children before the parent; used to evaluate arithmetic expressions represented as syntax trees (evaluate operands before the operator).
keywords.example: For node '+', evaluate left subtree, then right subtree, then perform addition.

understand.visual_alt: Two columns: Left lists compiler phases P (Lexical), Q (Parsing), R (Register allocation), S (Expression evaluation); Right lists techniques 1 (Graph coloring), 2 (DFA minimization), 3 (Post-order traversal), 4 (Production tree). Correct matches: P-2, Q-4, R-1, S-3.

given.aim: Identify which algorithmic technique or data structure corresponds to each compiler phase.

terms.meaning: First phase; converts source characters to tokens using finite automata built from regular expressions.
terms.connects: DFA minimization (2) is used to optimize the automaton for token recognition.

terms.meaning: Second phase; checks syntactic structure and builds a parse (production) tree.
terms.example: Parsing 'if (x) y;' to verify it matches the grammar.
terms.connects: A production tree (4) is the direct output of parsing.

terms.meaning: Back-end phase; assigns variables to a limited set of processor registers.
terms.example: Deciding which of x, y, z get registers vs. spill to memory.
terms.connects: Graph coloring (1) solves register allocation by coloring the interference graph.

terms.meaning: Evaluating an arithmetic/logical expression represented as a tree.
terms.connects: Post-order traversal (3) evaluates operands before operators, giving correct order.

given.plan: For each phase recall its defining algorithm: lexical analysis uses DFA (DFA minimization); parsing produces a production tree; register allocation uses graph coloring; expression evaluation over a tree uses post-order traversal.

steps.title: Match Lexical Analysis
steps.formula_raw: Regular expression -> NFA -> DFA -> Minimized DFA
steps.apply: Lexical analysis builds a scanner using finite automata. The classical optimisation step is DFA minimization. So P matches 2.
steps.note: DFA minimization reduces the number of states without changing the language accepted.

steps.title: Match Parsing
steps.apply: The parser applies grammar productions and records them in a production (parse) tree. So Q matches 4.
steps.note: A production tree directly reflects grammar rule applications.

steps.title: Match Register Allocation
steps.apply: Register allocation builds an interference graph (nodes = variables, edges = simultaneous liveness) and applies graph coloring (colors = registers). So R matches 1.
steps.note: This is the Chaitin-style register allocator used in real compilers.

steps.title: Match Expression Evaluation
steps.apply: To evaluate an expression tree, process both children before the root (post-order). So S matches 3.
steps.note: Post-order traversal naturally produces an evaluation order that satisfies data dependencies.

solution.result: P-2, Q-4, R-1, S-3 => Option B.

questions.formula_note: Key ideas: DFA minimization for lexical analysis; parse/production tree for parsing; graph coloring for register allocation; post-order tree traversal for expression evaluation.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(P) Lexical analysis       (i)  Leftmost derivation
(Q) Top-down parsing       (ii) Type checking
(R) Semantic analysis      (iii) Regular expressions
(S) Activation records     (iv)  Runtime administration

A. P -> iii, Q -> i, R -> ii, S -> iv
B. P -> ii, Q -> i, R -> iii, S -> iv
C. P -> iii, Q -> ii, R -> i, S -> iv
D. P -> iii, Q -> i, R -> iv, S -> ii
questions.answer: A. P -> iii, Q -> i, R -> ii, S -> iv
questions.to_find: Match each compiler concept (P, Q, R, S) to its associated technique or purpose (i, ii, iii, iv).

understand.plain: This question tests whether students know which concept belongs to which compiler phase. Each of the four items on the left is a phase or concept; each item on the right is a technique or purpose. You need to pair them correctly.

keywords.explain: A parsing strategy where the leftmost non-terminal is always expanded first; characteristic of top-down (predictive) parsers.
keywords.example: LL(1) parsers produce a leftmost derivation of the input string.

keywords.explain: A block of memory pushed onto the call stack when a function is called; holds parameters, local variables, return address, and saved registers.
keywords.example: Each recursive call to factorial pushes a new activation record.

understand.visual_alt: Four pairs: Lexical analysis uses regular expressions; Top-down parsing produces leftmost derivation; Semantic analysis performs type checking; Activation records are part of runtime administration.

given.aim: Correctly associate each compiler phase/concept with its defining technique or purpose.

terms.meaning: Scans source characters and produces tokens using patterns defined by regular expressions.
terms.connects: Regular expressions (iii) are the specification language for lexical tokens.

terms.meaning: Parsing that starts from the start symbol and tries to derive the input string; uses leftmost derivations.
terms.example: Recursive descent and LL parsers are top-down.
terms.connects: Top-down parsers produce leftmost derivation (i) at each step.

terms.meaning: Phase that checks meaning-level correctness such as type compatibility and scope rules.
terms.example: Checking that you cannot add an integer and a string without casting.
terms.connects: Type checking (ii) is the main task of semantic analysis.

terms.meaning: Stack frames created at runtime to manage function calls, local variables, and control flow.
terms.example: Each call to a function allocates an activation record on the stack.
terms.connects: Managing activation records is a runtime administration (iv) concern.

given.plan: Recall the defining feature of each phase: lexical -> regular expressions; top-down parsing -> leftmost derivation; semantic analysis -> type checking; activation records -> runtime administration.

steps.title: Lexical analysis uses regular expressions
steps.apply: Lexical analyzers are specified using regular expressions, then compiled to DFAs. P matches (iii).
steps.note: This is the most fundamental fact about lexical analysis.

steps.title: Top-down parsing produces leftmost derivation
steps.formula_raw: At each step replace leftmost non-terminal
steps.apply: By definition, top-down parsers expand the leftmost non-terminal, so they produce a leftmost derivation. Q matches (i).
steps.note: Bottom-up parsers produce rightmost derivation in reverse.

steps.title: Semantic analysis performs type checking
steps.apply: Semantic analysis goes beyond syntax to check type compatibility, scope, and other semantic rules. R matches (ii).
steps.note: Type checking is done after parsing, using the parse/AST tree.

steps.title: Activation records are runtime administration
steps.apply: Activation records are a runtime mechanism (not a compile-time phase). Managing them is runtime administration. S matches (iv).
steps.note: The compiler generates code to manage activation records, but the records exist at runtime.

solution.result: P -> iii, Q -> i, R -> ii, S -> iv => Option A.

questions.formula_note: Each compiler phase has a signature concept: lexical analysis -> regular expressions; top-down parsing -> leftmost derivation; semantic analysis -> type checking; activation records -> runtime administration.

questions.question:
Match the following according to input (from the left column) to the compiler phase (in the right column) that processes it:

P. Syntax tree                  i.  Code generator
Q. Character stream             ii. Syntax analyzer
R. Intermediate representation  iii. Semantic analyzer
S. Token stream                 iv. Lexical analyzer

A. P-iii, Q-iv, R-i, S-ii
B. P-iii, Q-i, R-ii, S-iv
C. P-ii, Q-iv, R-iii, S-i
D. P-iv, Q-iii, R-ii, S-i
questions.to_find: Identify which compiler phase takes each given data structure/stream as its input.

understand.plain: A compiler transforms data through multiple phases: characters -> tokens -> parse tree / AST -> annotated AST -> intermediate representation -> machine code. This question asks which phase reads each type of input. Think about the pipeline order.

keywords.explain: The raw sequence of characters in the source file; the very first input to a compiler.
keywords.example: The file 'int x = 5;' is a character stream: 'i','n','t',' ','x',' ','=',' ','5',';'.

keywords.explain: The sequence of lexical tokens produced by the lexical analyzer and consumed by the parser (syntax analyzer).
keywords.example: From 'int x = 5;' -> tokens: INT, IDENT(x), ASSIGN, NUM(5), SEMI.

keywords.explain: The parse tree or AST produced by the parser; consumed by the semantic analyzer to check meaning.
keywords.example: The AST for 'x + y' has a '+' node with children 'x' and 'y'.

keywords.explain: A machine-independent code form (e.g., three-address code) produced by semantic analysis; consumed by the code generator.
keywords.example: t1 = a + b; t2 = t1 * c is three-address code IR.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='560' height='140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='45' width='110' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Char stream</text><text x='60' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Q</text><polygon points='115,70 130,60 130,80' class='ok'/><rect x='130' y='45' width='110' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='185' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Lexical</text><text x='185' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Analyzer iv</text><polygon points='240,70 255,60 255,80' class='ok'/><rect x='255' y='45' width='110' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Token stream</text><text x='310' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>S</text><polygon points='365,70 380,60 380,80' class='ok'/><rect x='380' y='45' width='110' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='435' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Syntax</text><text x='435' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Analyzer ii</text><polygon points='490,70 505,60 505,80' class='ok'/><rect x='505' y='45' width='50' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='530' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>...</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Compiler pipeline: Character stream -> Lexical Analyzer (iv) -> Token stream (S) -> Syntax Analyzer (ii) -> Syntax tree (P) -> Semantic Analyzer (iii) -> IR (R) -> Code Generator (i). Q feeds Lexical Analyzer; S feeds Syntax Analyzer; P feeds Semantic Analyzer; R feeds Code Generator.

given.aim: For each input data form, identify which compiler phase consumes it.

terms.meaning: The raw source text before any tokenization.
terms.connects: The lexical analyzer (iv) reads the character stream and produces tokens.

terms.meaning: Sequence of tokens output by the lexical analyzer.
terms.example: IDENT, ASSIGN, NUM, PLUS, NUM, SEMI.
terms.connects: The syntax analyzer / parser (ii) reads tokens and builds a parse tree.

terms.meaning: Tree representation of the program structure built by the parser.
terms.example: AST node for assignment with children for LHS and RHS.
terms.connects: The semantic analyzer (iii) walks the syntax tree to check types and scopes.

terms.meaning: Machine-independent code produced by the front end (semantic analysis / IR generation).
terms.example: Three-address code like t1 = a + b.
terms.connects: The code generator (i) translates IR into target machine code.

given.plan: Follow the compilation pipeline order: characters -> lexical analyzer -> tokens -> syntax analyzer -> syntax tree -> semantic analyzer -> IR -> code generator. Each input is consumed by the next phase.

steps.title: Character stream input
steps.apply: The lexical analyzer (scanner) reads raw characters. Q (Character stream) -> iv (Lexical analyzer).
steps.note: The lexical analyzer is the first phase; it is the only one that processes raw characters.

steps.title: Token stream input
steps.apply: The syntax analyzer (parser) receives tokens from the lexical analyzer. S (Token stream) -> ii (Syntax analyzer).
steps.note: The parser uses the grammar to group tokens into a tree structure.

steps.title: Syntax tree input
steps.apply: The semantic analyzer receives the syntax/parse tree and performs type checking, scope resolution. P (Syntax tree) -> iii (Semantic analyzer).
steps.note: Semantic analysis decorates/annotates the AST with type information.

steps.title: Intermediate representation input
steps.apply: The code generator takes the IR and produces target machine code. R (Intermediate representation) -> i (Code generator).
steps.note: IR is machine-independent; the code generator handles machine-specific details.

solution.result: P-iii, Q-iv, R-i, S-ii => Option A.

questions.formula_note: Compiler pipeline order: Character stream -> Lexical Analyzer -> Token stream -> Syntax Analyzer -> Syntax tree -> Semantic Analyzer -> IR -> Code Generator.

questions.question:
Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

A. Context-free grammar can be used to specify both lexical and syntax rules
B. Type checking is done before parsing
C. High-level language programs can be translated to different Intermediate Representations
D. Arguments to a function can be passed using the program stack
questions.answer: B. Type checking is done before parsing
questions.to_find: Identify the FALSE statement about compiler design concepts.

understand.plain: Three of the four statements are true facts about compilers; one is false. You need to evaluate each statement against your knowledge of how compilers work. The false statement contradicts the standard compilation pipeline order.

keywords.explain: The semantic analysis phase where the compiler verifies that operations are applied to compatible types; it occurs AFTER parsing, not before.
keywords.example: Checking that 'x + y' is valid requires knowing the types of x and y, which are found only after the AST is built by the parser.

keywords.explain: A grammar formalism powerful enough to describe both simple regular languages (lexical patterns) and context-free languages (programming language syntax).
keywords.example: CFG can express both 'identifier = letter (letter | digit)*' and 'if-else' statement nesting.

understand.visual_alt: Compilation pipeline: Lexical analysis -> Parsing -> Semantic analysis (type checking) -> IR generation -> Optimization -> Code generation. Type checking comes AFTER parsing, making statement B false.

given.aim: Determine which statement about compiler phases is factually incorrect.

terms.meaning: CFG can specify lexical rules (regular grammars are a subset of CFGs) and syntax rules.
terms.example: A regular grammar for identifiers is a valid CFG.
terms.connects: TRUE - CFGs are a superset of regular grammars, so they can express lexical rules.

terms.meaning: Claims type checking happens before parsing.
terms.example: In reality: source -> lex -> parse -> type check.
terms.connects: FALSE - type checking is part of semantic analysis which occurs AFTER parsing.

terms.meaning: A high-level language can be compiled to different IR forms (AST, three-address code, SSA, stack-based IR, etc.).
terms.example: Java compiles to JVM bytecode; GCC uses GIMPLE IR; LLVM uses LLVM IR.
terms.connects: TRUE - multiple IR choices exist and compilers may use different ones.

terms.meaning: Function arguments can be passed via the program stack (call stack), using activation records.
terms.example: In C calling convention, arguments are pushed onto the stack before calling the function.
terms.connects: TRUE - stack-based argument passing is a standard and common technique.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement against the standard compilation pipeline. The pipeline order is: lexical analysis -> parsing -> semantic analysis (type checking) -> IR generation -> code generation. Any statement that contradicts this order is false.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement A
steps.formula_raw: Regular grammar is a subset of CFG
steps.apply: Context-free grammars can represent regular languages, so they can specify both lexical rules (regular patterns) and syntax rules (context-free rules). Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: In practice, separate tools (Lex/Flex for lexical, Yacc/Bison for syntax) are used, but both are expressible via CFG.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement B
steps.apply: Type checking requires the program to be syntactically parsed first (an AST or parse tree must exist). Therefore type checking is done AFTER parsing, not before. Statement B is FALSE.
steps.note: This is the key answer - type checking is a semantic analysis task, which comes after syntactic parsing.

steps.title: Evaluate Statements C and D
steps.apply: Statement C is TRUE: compilers can target different IRs (LLVM IR, JVM bytecode, three-address code). Statement D is TRUE: the call stack is a standard mechanism for passing arguments to functions.
steps.note: Both C and D describe well-known, standard compiler/runtime techniques.

solution.result: Statement B is FALSE. Type checking is done AFTER parsing (not before).

questions.formula_note: Standard compilation pipeline order: Lexical analysis -> Parsing -> Semantic analysis (includes type checking) -> IR generation -> Optimization -> Code generation. Type checking always follows parsing.
